root.narration: The set is tied 6-6 and you are in a tiebreak. You are serving at 5-4, which means you only need two more points to win the set. Your opponent is nervous. What is your approach?

choices.text: Stay aggressive on your serve, aim for the corners, and play high-percentage tennis on the return points -- no unnecessary risks
Play it safe on every point -- just get the ball in and hope your opponent makes mistakes
Try to end every point as fast as possible by going for huge shots because you are nervous too

outcome.headline: Tiebreak champion!
outcome.explanation: By being aggressive on your serve and smart on the return points, you won 7-4. You did not give away free points but you also did not let your opponent off the hook.
outcome.whatToRemember: In tiebreaks, be aggressive on your serve points but disciplined on return points. Every point matters more than in a regular game, so pick your spots wisely.

keyTerms.definition: A special game played when the set reaches 6-6. The first player serves one point, then players alternate serving every two points. The first to reach 7 points with a 2-point lead wins the set

outcome.headline: Too passive -- lost momentum!
outcome.explanation: Playing safe let your opponent gain confidence. They started hitting bigger shots and you had no answer because you were just pushing the ball back. The tiebreak slipped away 7-5.
outcome.whatToRemember: Being safe is not the same as being smart. You still need to put pressure on your opponent, especially on your serve points.

outcome.headline: Errors everywhere!
outcome.explanation: Swinging for the fences on every point led to three straight unforced errors. Your 5-4 lead turned into a 5-7 loss. Tiebreaks reward discipline, not desperation.
outcome.whatToRemember: Nerves make you want to end points quickly, but that leads to errors. Take a deep breath, stick to your game plan, and make your opponent earn every point.
